content.content:
330	            sort the dict by key, case insensitive, reverse order
331	
332	        {% for key, value in mydict|dictsort(true) %}
333	            sort the dict by key, case sensitive
334	
335	        {% for key, value in mydict|dictsort(false, 'value') %}
336	            sort the dict by value, case insensitive
337	    """
338	    if by == "key":
339	        pos = 0
340	    elif by == "value":
341	        pos = 1
342	    else:
343	        raise FilterArgumentError('You can only sort by either "key" or "value"')
344	
345	    def sort_func(item: t.Tuple[t.Any, t.Any]) -> t.Any:
346	        value = item[pos]
347	
348	        if not case_sensitive:
349	            value = ignore_case(value)
350	
351	        return value
352	
353	    return sorted(value.items(), key=sort_func, reverse=reverse)
354	
355	
356	@pass_environment
357	def do_sort(
358	    environment: "Environment",
359	    value: "t.Iterable[V]",
360	    reverse: bool = False,
361	    case_sensitive: bool = False,
362	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None,
363	) -> "t.List[V]":
364	    """Sort an iterable using Python's :func:`sorted`.
365	
366	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
367	
368	        {% for city in cities|sort %}
369	            ...
370	        {% endfor %}
371	
372	    :param reverse: Sort descending instead of ascending.
373	    :param case_sensitive: When sorting strings, sort upper and lower
374	        case separately.
375	    :param attribute: When sorting objects or dicts, an attribute or
376	        key to sort by. Can use dot notation like ``"address.city"``.
377	        Can be a list of attributes like ``"age,name"``.
378	
379	    The sort is stable, it does not change the relative order of
380	    elements that compare equal. This makes it is possible to chain
381	    sorts on different attributes and ordering.
382	
383	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
384	
385	        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="name")
386	            |sort(reverse=true, attribute="age") %}
387	            ...
388	        {% endfor %}
389	
390	    As a shortcut to chaining when the direction is the same for all
391	    attributes, pass a comma separate list of attributes.
392	
393	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
394	
395	        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="age,name") %}
396	            ...
397	        {% endfor %}
398	
399	    .. versionchanged:: 2.11.0
400	        The ``attribute`` parameter can be a comma separated list of
401	        attributes, e.g. ``"age,name"``.
402	
403	    .. versionchanged:: 2.6
404	       The ``attribute`` parameter was added.
405	    """
406	    key_func = make_multi_attrgetter(
407	        environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None
408	    )
409	    return sorted(value, key=key_func, reverse=reverse)
410	
411	
412	@pass_environment
413	def do_unique(
414	    environment: "Environment",
415	    value: "t.Iterable[V]",
416	    case_sensitive: bool = False,
417	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None,
418	) -> "t.Iterator[V]":
419	    """Returns a list of unique items from the given iterable.
420	
421	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
422	
423	        {{ ['foo', 'bar', 'foobar', 'FooBar']|unique|list }}
424	            -> ['foo', 'bar', 'foobar']
425	
426	    The unique items are yielded in the same order as their first occurrence in
427	    the iterable passed to the filter.
428	
429	    :param case_sensitive: Treat upper and lower case strings as distinct.
430	    :param attribute: Filter objects with unique values for this attribute.
431	    """
432	    getter = make_attrgetter(
433	        environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None
434	    )
435	    seen = set()
436	
437	    for item in value:
438	        key = getter(item)
439	
440	        if key not in seen:
441	            seen.add(key)
442	            yield item
443	
444	
445	def _min_or_max(
446	    environment: "Environment",
447	    value: "t.Iterable[V]",
448	    func: "t.Callable[..., V]",
449	    case_sensitive: bool,
450	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]],
451	) -> "t.Union[V, Undefined]":
452	    it = iter(value)
453	
454	    try:
455	        first = next(it)
456	    except StopIteration:
457	        return environment.undefined("No aggregated item, sequence was empty.")
458	
459	    key_func = make_attrgetter(
460	        environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None
461	    )
462	    return func(chain([first], it), key=key_func)
463

468	    value: "t.Iterable[V]",
469	    case_sensitive: bool = False,

The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231 1232 1233 @pass_environment 1234 def sync_do_sum( 1235 environment: "Environment", 1236 iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", 1237 attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, 1238 start: V = 0, # type: ignore 1239 ) -> V:

content.content:
ugrep: warning: /workspace/repo/pyproject.toml: No such file or directory
.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer
        supported. Override ``resolve_or_missing`` instead of
        ``resolve``.
    -   ``unicode_urlencode`` is renamed to ``url_quote``.

-   Add support for native types in macros. :issue:`1510`
-   The ``{% trans %}`` tag can use ``pgettext`` and ``npgettext`` by
    passing a context string as the first token in the tag, like
    ``{% trans "title" %}``. :issue:`1430`
-   Update valid identifier characters from Python 3.6 to 3.7.
    :pr:`1571`
-   Filters and tests decorated with ``@async_variant`` are pickleable.
    :pr:`1612`
-   Add ``items`` filter. :issue:`1561`
-   Subscriptions (``[0]``, etc.) can be used after filters, tests, and
    calls when the environment is in async mode. :issue:`1573`


Version 3.0.3
-------------

.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer
        supported. Override ``resolve_or_missing`` instead of
        ``resolve``.
    -   ``unicode_urlencode`` is renamed to ``url_quote``.

-   Add support for native types in macros. :issue:`1510`
-   The ``{% trans %}`` tag can use ``pgettext`` and ``npgettext`` by
    passing a context string as the first token in the tag, like
    ``{% trans "title" %}``. :issue:`1430`
-   Update valid identifier characters from Python 3.6 to 3.7.
    :pr:`1571`
-   Filters and tests decorated with ``@async_variant`` are pickleable.
    :pr:`1612`
-   Add ``items`` filter. :issue:`1561`
-   Subscriptions (``[0]``, etc.) can be used after filters, tests, and
    calls when the environment is in async mode. :issue:`1573`


Version 3.0.3
-------------

content.text: Now let's implement both parameters in the groupby filter.

input.old_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, include_empty: bool = True, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will use the case of the first item encountered for that value. For example, if a list of items has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the ``"CA"`` and ``"ca"`` entries will be grouped together, under a group named ``"CA"``. This can be disabled by passing ``case_sensitive=True``. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", case_sensitive=true) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can use ``include_empty=False`` to exclude groups where the attribute is ``None``, an empty string, or another falsy value. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", include_empty=false) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` and ``include_empty`` parameters. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if case_sensitive: key_func = expr else: key_func = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case ) if not include_empty: value = [item for item in value if expr(item)] out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=key_func), key_func): group_items = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(group_items[0]), group_items)) return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, include_empty: bool = True, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if case_sensitive: key_func = expr else: key_func = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case ) value = await auto_to_list(value) if not include_empty: value = [item for item in value if expr(item)] out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=key_func), key_func): group_items = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(group_items[0]), group_items)) return out

@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
tool_use_result.newString:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, include_empty: bool = True, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will use the case of the first item encountered for that value. For example, if a list of items has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the ``"CA"`` and ``"ca"`` entries will be grouped together, under a group named ``"CA"``. This can be disabled by passing ``case_sensitive=True``. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", case_sensitive=true) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can use ``include_empty=False`` to exclude groups where the attribute is ``None``, an empty string, or another falsy value. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", include_empty=false) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` and ``include_empty`` parameters. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if case_sensitive: key_func = expr else: key_func = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case ) if not include_empty: value = [item for item in value if expr(item)] out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=key_func), key_func): group_items = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(group_items[0]), group_items)) return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, include_empty: bool = True, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if case_sensitive: key_func = expr else: key_func = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case ) value = await auto_to_list(value) if not include_empty: value = [item for item in value if expr(item)] out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=key_func), key_func): group_items = list(group) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(group_items[0]), group_items)) return out

content.text: Now let's verify with a quick test.

input.command:
python3 -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()

class U:
    def __init__(self, city, name):
        self.city = city
        self.name = name

users = [U('CA','a'), U('NY','b'), U('ca','c'), U(None,'d'), U('','e')]

tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}')
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", case_sensitive=true) %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))

tmpl3 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", include_empty=false) %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}')
print(tmpl3.render(users=users))
"

input.command:
python3 -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()

class U:
    def __init__(self, city, name):
        self.city = city
        self.name = name

users = [U('CA','a'), U('NY','b'), U('ca','c')]

tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}')
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", case_sensitive=true) %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))

users2 = users + [U(None,'d'), U('','e')]
tmpl3 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", include_empty=false) %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}')
print(tmpl3.render(users=users2))
"

content.text: Works as expected: case-insensitive grouping merges "CA"/"ca" under first-seen case, `case_sensitive=True` keeps them separate, and `include_empty=False` filters out falsy values (avoiding the None-sorting crash too). Now let's run the existing test suite for groupby.

content.text:
The diff is a clean single-file change to `src/jinja2/filters.py`, implementing both features:

1. **`case_sensitive`** (default `False`) — matches `sort()`'s default behavior; sorts/groups using `ignore_case`-normalized keys but reports the grouper using the original-case value from the first item in each group.
2. **`include_empty`** (default `True`) — filters out items whose grouping attribute is falsy (`None`, `""`, etc.) before sorting/grouping, which also incidentally avoids `TypeError` crashes from comparing `None` to `str` during sort.

Both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby` variant were updated identically. All existing groupby tests pass, and manual verification confirms case-insensitive grouping, `case_sensitive=True` opt-out, and `include_empty=False` filtering all work correctly. The diff is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.

result:
The diff is a clean single-file change to `src/jinja2/filters.py`, implementing both features:

1. **`case_sensitive`** (default `False`) — matches `sort()`'s default behavior; sorts/groups using `ignore_case`-normalized keys but reports the grouper using the original-case value from the first item in each group.
2. **`include_empty`** (default `True`) — filters out items whose grouping attribute is falsy (`None`, `""`, etc.) before sorting/grouping, which also incidentally avoids `TypeError` crashes from comparing `None` to `str` during sort.

Both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby` variant were updated identically. All existing groupby tests pass, and manual verification confirms case-insensitive grouping, `case_sensitive=True` opt-out, and `include_empty=False` filtering all work correctly. The diff is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.
